perturbation.original_text: 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and who is bound by confidentiality terms at least as protective of the Confidential Information as this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, without the need to be bound by confidentiality terms.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by removing the requirement for Authorized Persons to be bound by confidentiality terms. This directly contradicts clause 3.4 which states that Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to any third party , except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons on a need -to-know basis . Thus, this create ambiguity, since this section doesn't mention they should be held to confidentially terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to any third party , except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons on a need -to-know basis .
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 1.1 removes the requirement for 'Authorized Persons' to be bound by confidentiality terms, which directly contradicts section 3.4, which states that each party shall hold the other party's Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other party's Confidential Information to any third party, except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons. Thus, this creates an inconsistency regarding whether 'Authorized Persons' are subject to confidentiality obligations, thereby creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information and protect it from access by anyone other than its Authorized Persons, in the same way that it protects its own Confidential Information, but in all cases using a professional degree of care . The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 5 (five) years after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information and protect it from access by anyone other than its Authorized Persons, in the same way that it protects its own Confidential Information, but in all cases using a reasonable degree of care . The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 1 (one) year after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The modification lowers the standard of care from 'professional' to 'reasonable' and reduces the protection period from 5 years to 1 year. This introduces an in-text contradiction because while reasonable care is now sufficient under 3.5, clause 2.2 states that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm, implying a higher standard of care is expected. Therefore, changing the first to reasonable degree of care and also the period to 1 year instead of 5 years, creates an in-text contradiction, because this could not possibly prevent irreperable harm.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the standard of care for confidential information from 'professional' to 'reasonable' and the protection period from 5 years to 1 year. This directly contradicts section 2.2, which states that a breach of confidentiality may cause 'irreparable harm' and allows for injunctive relief. 'Reasonable care' and a 1-year protection period are less likely to prevent 'irreparable harm' than 'professional' care and a 5-year period, creating a direct conflict in the expected level of protection and potential consequences. This fulfills the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' since different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.8. Return of Confidential Information . Immediately upon the Disclosing Party ’s request or by the last date defined in clause 3.2, the Receiving Party shall return /or destroy, at the Disclosing Party ’s discretion and expense, all the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its possession, irrespective of the media, and certify execution of this obligation in writing. This obligation shall not apply to (i) copies retained in compliance with a Party’s legal or regulatory obligations or (ii) copies made as part of preexisting data back -up proce sses, if such copies a re not accessible to the Receiving Party’s st aff or Authorized Persons in the normal course of business.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.8. Return of Confidential Information . Immediately upon the Disclosing Party ’s request, the Receiving Party may elect to return /or destroy, at the Receiving Party ’s discretion and expense, all the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its possession, irrespective of the media, and certify execution of this obligation in writing. This obligation shall not apply to (i) copies retained in compliance with a Party’s legal or regulatory obligations or (ii) copies made as part of preexisting data back -up proce sses, if such copies a re not accessible to the Receiving Party’s st aff or Authorized Persons in the normal course of business.
perturbation.explanation: The modification makes the return/destruction of Confidential Information optional ('may elect') rather than mandatory ('shall'). This creates an in-text contradiction with the intent of the agreement to protect Confidential Information, specified in the Purpose (Section 1). If the Receiving Party can simply choose not to return or destroy confidential information, it undermines the entire basis of the NDA, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Purpose The Parties are contemplating, negotiating, executing or have already entered into one or more agreements relating to testing computer software between the Parties, which may lead to the exchange of information that they wish to protect and whose use they wish to limit through this Agreement (the “ Purpose ”).
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates ('shall') the return or destruction of confidential information, reinforcing the Purpose (Section 1) of protecting and limiting the use of that information. The changed text makes this return/destruction optional ('may elect'), directly contradicting the agreement's purpose by allowing the Receiving Party to retain confidential information at will. This undermines the very foundation of the NDA, creating a strong in-text contradiction with Section 1.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Hungary , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause minor harm to the other and that actual damages may be easily ascertain or adequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by financial compensation, specific performance, or other equitable relief, with a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The modification alters the governing law to Hungary and changes the perceived harm from 'irreparable' to 'minor,' also changing actual damages from 'difficult to ascertain or inadequate' to 'easily ascertain or adequate'. This alteration creates an in-text contradiction. The agreement's purpose (Section 1) is to protect information. Stating the harm is 'minor' and damages are 'easily ascertainable' contradicts this core purpose, as it minimizes the importance of protecting the confidential information, implying it isn't truly valuable or sensitive if breaches cause only minor harm.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Purpose The Parties are contemplating, negotiating, executing or have already entered into one or more agreements relating to testing computer software between the Parties, which may lead to the exchange of information that they wish to protect and whose use they wish to limit through this Agreement (the “ Purpose ”).
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law to Hungary and significantly downplays the potential harm from a breach of confidentiality, stating it would cause 'minor harm' and that damages are 'easily ascertainable.' This directly contradicts Section 1 (Purpose), which emphasizes the parties' intent to protect information and limit its use, implying the information is highly valuable and sensitive. By stating the harm is 'minor,' the modified text undermines the very purpose of the agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The original text aligns with Section 1, but the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7. Required Disclosures . Nothing in this Agreement shall prohibit either Party from disclosing a part of the other Party’s Confidential Information if legally required to do so by law or regulation (including those governing capital markets), judicial or governmental order , provided that it gives, to the extent and as soon as legally permissible, prior notice of such disclosure to the other Party .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7. Required Disclosures . Nothing in this Agreement shall prohibit either Party from disclosing a part of the other Party’s Confidential Information if legally required to do so by law or regulation (including those governing capital markets), judicial or governmental order , without giving prior notice of such disclosure to the other Party .
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for prior notice before disclosing confidential information due to legal requirements. This creates an in-text contradiction with clause 3.4 which obligates each party to hold the other party’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence. By removing the prior notice requirement, it allows for potential breaches of confidentiality without the Disclosing Party being aware, therefore undermining the obligation of holding information in strictest confidence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to any third party , except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons on a need -to-know basis .
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'prior notice' requirement from clause 3.7, while clause 3.4 mandates holding confidential information in 'strictest confidence' and restricts disclosure. By removing the prior notice, 3.7 allows for potential breaches of confidentiality without informing the Disclosing Party, thereby contradicting the 'strictest confidence' obligation in 3.4. This creates a direct conflict as it undermines the protection of confidential information outlined in 3.4, fitting the definition of an inconsistency leading to an in-text contradiction.
